context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Global Poverty

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How has development come to be associated popularly with poverty alleviation? What do we make of techniques of poverty alleviation such as microfinance, in terms of the theories of economic development? Is it possible to focus on poverty successfully in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism? Are the techniques for measuring poverty, and the goal-setting exercised like MDG, satisfactory?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m kinda lost on this global poverty reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole prompt from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Hey! Happy to help you work through this. Before we dive in, let me ask you: when you hear the word 'development' in the context of a country, what's the first thing that comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole prompt, which would essentially do the work for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them engage with the prompt themselves. I'll start by asking them what part specifically feels confusing, or what they already know about development and poverty, to activate prior knowledge and get them thinking rather than passively receiving information.

exchanges.student: idk rn, maybe like helping poor countries? but ngl i’m confused about all of it.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a pretty natural starting point! Now think about this: do you think 'development' has always meant poverty alleviation, or do you think that association came about at some specific point in history? What's your gut feeling?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a reasonable starting intuition - development as 'helping poor countries.' This is actually a good entry point. Rather than explaining everything, I should build on this and push them to think more critically about the first part of the prompt: how did development come to be associated with poverty alleviation? I'll ask a targeted question to get them to think about whether development always meant poverty alleviation, or if this association came about at some point in history.

exchanges.student: uh maybe it changed over time? can u just give me the big picture first bc i’m not seeing how any of this fits together.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct that it changed over time is a good one - trust that! So let's start there: what kinds of events or moments in history do you think might have shifted how people define 'development'? Think about what you've covered in class so far.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the big picture, which would be doing their thinking for them. I should resist this and instead guide them to build their own understanding piece by piece. Their instinct that 'it changed over time' is actually correct and worth building on. I'll acknowledge that and ask them to think about what might have caused that shift, keeping things bite-sized.

exchanges.student: tbh that’s where i’m stuck, like maybe colonialism or globalization or smth? i just need the overall explanation first.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but giving you the overall explanation isn't my role here - that's your job as the writer! The good news is you already named two really relevant concepts: colonialism and globalization. Pick one. What do you think that concept has to do with how rich countries started thinking about 'developing' poorer ones?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for an overall explanation, which I should not provide. However, they've actually named two very relevant concepts - colonialism and globalization - which are both touched on in the syllabus. I should acknowledge these as good starting points and push them to think more specifically about one of them rather than giving them the big picture. I'll be direct that I won't give them the overview, and redirect them to work with what they already have.

exchanges.student: maybe colonialism messed things up and then development became about fixing poverty? idk if that’s even right, i’m lost again.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable direction - you're onto something. Now be more specific: who do you think started framing development as poverty alleviation, and why? Was it the formerly colonized countries themselves, or someone else pushing that agenda?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuine intellectual connection - colonialism created conditions of poverty, and development became framed as addressing that. This is actually a solid starting intuition that connects to the course themes around colonial legacies. I should affirm the direction without confirming it as complete, and push them to be more specific about the mechanism. I want to keep them moving forward rather than letting them spiral into feeling lost.

exchanges.student: idk who exactly, maybe governments or the UN or aid groups? can u pls just explain that part straight up.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually named key actors there - the UN, aid organizations, governments. Here's a sharper question: why would those actors benefit from framing development as poverty alleviation specifically? What's in it for them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a direct explanation. I should refuse but acknowledge they've listed some genuinely relevant actors. I'll push them to think about the motivations of these actors, which connects to the course theme of external actors shaping development planning. I want to keep things bite-sized and move them toward being able to write something.

exchanges.student: maybe bc it sounds moral? but then i get confused bc the prompt also wants microfinance and MDGs and hyper-capitalism, so i kinda need the full map first.
exchanges.tutor: The 'moral framing' point is actually quite sharp - hold onto that. But I want to be direct: I won't map out the full prompt for you. You already have enough to start writing. The prompt has several questions - you don't need to grasp them all simultaneously. Why not try writing 2-3 sentences on just the first question using what you've figured out so far? That often makes the rest clearer.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps circling back to wanting a full map/explanation, and is now showing some frustration with the complexity of the prompt. This is a redundant pattern emerging. I should acknowledge their 'sounds moral' point as genuinely insightful, then redirect them to recognize that the prompt has multiple questions and they don't need to understand it all at once. I should encourage them to start writing with what they have rather than waiting for full clarity. I also want to be direct that I won't map it all out for them.

exchanges.student: i can’t really rn bc i don’t know if my angle is wrong. can u at least tell me what each part of the prompt is basically asking?
exchanges.tutor: Your angle isn't wrong - you've made genuinely good observations in this conversation. The hesitation is holding you back more than any gap in knowledge. Here's my challenge: take your 'moral framing' idea and your point about colonialism and write just 2-3 sentences right now. Don't worry about being perfect. Show me what you have and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still resisting writing and looking for reassurance or a roadmap. This is now a clear redundant pattern - they keep asking for explanations in different forms. I should be firm but encouraging, and push them to just attempt something. Their angle isn't wrong - they've made good points. I should challenge them directly to commit to writing rather than continuing to seek validation.

exchanges.student: ok but fr i still don’t know what the microfinance/theories part means. that part especially is confusing.
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough, let's look at that. What do you already know about microfinance? Even just the basics of how it works?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking about a specific part of the prompt - microfinance and theories of economic development - which is a more focused question than before. This is actually progress. I should engage with this but use the Socratic method rather than explaining it. I'll ask them what they know about microfinance first to see what they're working with, keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: just tiny loans to poor ppl i think, but idk why that connects to economic development theory at all.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's the right basic idea. So here's the tension: if development theory is about how entire economies grow, why would giving small loans to individuals be considered a development strategy? What does that assume about how poverty gets solved?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a basic definition of microfinance right. Now I should push them to think about the connection to economic development theory through a Socratic question. The key tension here is between microfinance as an individual-level intervention versus broader structural theories of economic development (like industrialization, capital accumulation, etc.). I want to get them to see this tension themselves.

deductions.reason: In Turn 9, when the student flags specific confusion about microfinance and economic theory, the tutor asks a diagnostic question ('What do you already know about microfinance?') which is appropriate. However, in earlier turns (3-6) when the student repeatedly signals being lost and needing an overview, the tutor jumps straight to redirecting or challenging rather than first diagnosing what specifically the student does and does not understand about the prompt structure. The tutor does not probe to find the precise gap before pushing the student to write.

The tutor affirms the student's ideas ('that's a reasonable direction,' 'that's actually quite sharp') but does not provide specific method-level feedback on how the student should structure their thinking or approach the reflection assignment. Feedback like 'your angle isn't wrong' is too vague to improve the student's analytical process or writing methodology.

Across Turns 3 through 8, the student repeatedly requests an overview or map of the prompt and the tutor repeatedly declines and redirects to writing 2-3 sentences. The same move (refuse overview, push to write) is repeated across multiple turns without synthesis, reframing, or a different approach to break the cycle. The student remains stuck and the tutor does not switch strategy meaningfully until Turn 9.

grade.overview: The tutor does well at avoiding giving direct answers or submission-ready content, consistently using Socratic questioning and refusing to map out the prompt. The tutor also maintains a supportive tone and keeps responses concise. However, the tutor falls into a repetitive pattern across several turns—declining to give an overview and pushing the student to write—without adapting strategy when the student remains stuck. There is also a lack of specific method-level feedback on how to approach the reflection analytically. The 1.2.B.a deduction is applied with some uncertainty since the tutor does ask diagnostic questions in some turns, but the pattern of jumping to redirection before fully diagnosing the student's confusion is evident in the middle turns.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, rewrites student text, or gives conclusions before student reasoning. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because in the middle turns the tutor repeatedly redirects without first diagnosing what specifically the student does not understand about the prompt. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b because affirmations like 'your angle isn't wrong' and 'that's a reasonable direction' are too vague to improve the student's analytical method. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the tutor cycles through the same refusal-and-redirect move across Turns 3–8 without reframing, synthesizing, or switching strategy when the student remains stuck. 2.2 and section 3 have no deductions as the tutor stays on-task, maintains appropriate tone, and uses clear, bite-sized responses.
